name: Liu Bei
description: Liu Bei, also known as Emperor Zhaolie of Han, is a significant figure in Chinese mythology and history, particularly revered in Taoism and folk religion. He is traditionally considered the founder of the Shu Han dynasty during the Three Kingdoms period (220–280 CE). While a historical figure, Liu Bei's image has been significantly mythologized over centuries, transforming him into a benevolent and virtuous ruler embodying Confucian ideals of righteousness, benevolence, and filial piety. He is often depicted as a compassionate leader dedicated to restoring the Han dynasty and alleviating the suffering of the common people. Popular depictions portray him with exaggerated features of kindness and wisdom, solidifying his status as a deity-like figure. His role in the Romance of the Three Kingdoms further solidified his legendary status, cementing his place in the pantheon of popular Chinese deities and cultural heroes.
subtitle: Emperor Zhaolie of Han

keyMyths.title: The Oath of the Peach Garden
keyMyths.description: This legendary event recounts the sworn brotherhood between Liu Bei, Guan Yu, and Zhang Fei. They pledged allegiance to each other, vowing to work together to restore peace and prosperity to the land. This oath symbolizes their unwavering loyalty and commitment to justice, becoming a cornerstone of Liu Bei's heroic image and a powerful symbol of brotherhood in Chinese culture. The peach garden itself is often depicted as a sacred space where divine bonds are forged.
keyMyths.source: Romance of the Three Kingdoms, Chapter 1

keyMyths.title: Zhuge Liang's Allegiance
keyMyths.description: The story of Liu Bei's three visits to the thatched cottage of Zhuge Liang is a testament to his wisdom and humility. Despite his imperial lineage, Liu Bei patiently sought the counsel of the reclusive strategist. Zhuge Liang's acceptance of Liu Bei's offer marked a turning point, providing him with the strategic genius necessary to establish the Shu Han dynasty. This act solidified Liu Bei's reputation as a discerning and virtuous leader.
keyMyths.source: Romance of the Three Kingdoms, Chapter 38

keyMyths.title: The Battle of Changban
keyMyths.description: During the chaotic retreat from Changban, Liu Bei demonstrated his unwavering commitment to his people. Despite the overwhelming odds, he refused to abandon the civilians who followed him. This act of selflessness, even at the risk of his own life and the safety of his family, cemented his image as a compassionate ruler who placed the well-being of his subjects above all else. This battle is often depicted as a moment of divine intervention protecting Liu Bei.
keyMyths.source: Romance of the Three Kingdoms, Chapter 42

sources.author: Luo Guanzhong
sources.description: A historical novel that greatly romanticizes and mythologizes Liu Bei's life and deeds.
sources.text: A historical novel that greatly romanticizes and mythologizes Liu Bei's life and deeds.

sources.author: Chen Shou
sources.description: A more historical account of the Three Kingdoms period, providing a factual basis for Liu Bei's life.
sources.text: A more historical account of the Three Kingdoms period, providing a factual basis for Liu Bei's life.

sources.author: Rafe de Crespigny
sources.work: A Biographical Dictionary of Later Han to the Three Kingdoms (23-220 AD)
sources.description: Provides biographical information on historical figures of the period, including Liu Bei, offering a scholarly perspective.
sources.text: Provides biographical information on historical figures of the period, including Liu Bei, offering a scholarly perspective.

associations.name: Benevolence
associations.significance: Liu Bei is primarily associated with benevolence (仁), a core Confucian virtue. He is often depicted as a compassionate ruler who cares deeply for his people, earning him their loyalty and support.

associations.name: Righteousness
associations.significance: Liu Bei is seen as a symbol of righteousness (義), particularly in his dedication to restoring the Han dynasty. He consistently acted according to moral principles, even when faced with adversity.

associations.name: Legitimacy
associations.significance: As a descendant of the Han imperial family, Liu Bei represented the continuation of the Han dynasty's legitimacy, a crucial factor in his ability to rally support and establish his own kingdom.

associations.name: Brotherhood
associations.significance: The oath of brotherhood taken with Guan Yu and Zhang Fei is a central element of Liu Bei's legend, symbolizing loyalty, camaraderie, and shared destiny. It is a cornerstone of his heroic image.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: In some interpretations, Liu Bei is associated with the element of water, representing adaptability, flexibility, and the ability to overcome obstacles through persistence and resilience.

associations.name: Filial Piety
associations.significance: While not as prominent as other virtues, Liu Bei's commitment to restoring the Han dynasty can be seen as an extension of filial piety towards his ancestors, further solidifying his moral standing.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both Aeneas and Liu Bei are portrayed as virtuous leaders destined to found a new kingdom after a period of turmoil, emphasizing their lineage and moral authority.
Like King Arthur, Liu Bei is a charismatic leader who gathers loyal companions to fight for a noble cause, embodying ideals of chivalry, justice, and the restoration of a golden age.

Both Odysseus and Liu Bei are known for their strategic thinking, resilience, and ability to navigate complex political landscapes to achieve their goals, often relying on their wit and the loyalty of their followers.

Similar to Odin, Liu Bei possessed wisdom and sought knowledge, consulting with advisors and making strategic decisions based on his understanding of the political landscape. Both figures valued counsel and foresight.

worshipPractices.name: Ancestral Veneration
worshipPractices.description: As an emperor and a respected historical figure, Liu Bei is often venerated as an ancestor in family shrines and temples. Offerings and prayers are made to honor his memory and seek his blessings, particularly for success and prosperity. This practice is deeply rooted in Chinese traditions of ancestor worship.

worshipPractices.name: Temple Devotion
worshipPractices.description: Temples dedicated to Liu Bei, often alongside Guan Yu and Zhang Fei, exist throughout China and in Chinese communities worldwide. These temples serve as centers for religious and cultural activities, where devotees offer incense, perform rituals, and celebrate his virtues of benevolence and righteousness.

cultural.modernLegacy: Liu Bei remains a potent symbol of leadership, benevolence, and righteousness in modern Chinese culture. He is frequently portrayed in literature, opera, film, and video games, continuing to inspire audiences with his commitment to justice and his unwavering dedication to his people. His story serves as a moral compass, emphasizing the importance of integrity and compassion in leadership.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Aspirations
extendedContent.content: Born in Zhuo County (present-day Zhuozhou, Hebei), Liu Bei claimed descent from a branch of the Han imperial family, a lineage that would later become crucial to his legitimacy. His early life was marked by hardship, as he lost his father at a young age and had to support himself and his mother through menial labor, including weaving mats and selling shoes. Despite these humble beginnings, Liu Bei harbored grand ambitions. He was known for his charisma and attracted the attention of individuals who would later become his key allies, most notably Guan Yu and Zhang Fei. The three swore an oath of brotherhood in a peach garden, pledging loyalty and mutual support, a bond that would define much of Liu Bei's career. This early period reveals Liu Bei's inherent leadership qualities and his unwavering belief in his destiny to restore the Han dynasty, setting the stage for his future endeavors.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests and Battles
extendedContent.content: Liu Bei's quest to establish his own kingdom was fraught with challenges and setbacks. He initially served under various warlords, including Gongsun Zan and Tao Qian, demonstrating his military and administrative capabilities. However, he consistently faced betrayal and political maneuvering, forcing him to constantly seek new alliances and territories. A pivotal moment in his career was his acquisition of Jing Province (present-day Hubei and Hunan), which provided him with a strategic base of operations. His alliance with Sun Quan against Cao Cao at the Battle of Red Cliffs was a turning point, preventing Cao Cao from unifying China under his rule. Despite numerous defeats and periods of exile, Liu Bei never abandoned his ambition. His determination and unwavering belief in his cause inspired his followers and allowed him to eventually establish the Shu Han dynasty, claiming the imperial title and continuing the lineage of the Han emperors.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Cultural Impact
extendedContent.content: Liu Bei's legacy extends far beyond his historical achievements. He is revered as a symbol of benevolence, righteousness, and the embodiment of Confucian ideals. His story, immortalized in the Romance of the Three Kingdoms, continues to captivate audiences and shape cultural values. Liu Bei's emphasis on treating his people with compassion and his unwavering loyalty to his allies have made him a role model for leaders throughout Chinese history. His image is frequently invoked in political discourse, literature, and art, representing the virtues of just governance and moral leadership. The oath of brotherhood he swore with Guan Yu and Zhang Fei has become a powerful symbol of loyalty and camaraderie, influencing social norms and inspiring countless acts of selflessness and mutual support. Temples dedicated to Liu Bei and his sworn brothers stand as testaments to their enduring influence on Chinese culture and spirituality.

shortDescription: A benevolent ruler and founder of the Shu Han dynasty, Liu Bei is revered for his righteousness and compassion. His story embodies loyalty and just leadership.
